text: This is a case of a laparoscopic subtotal colectomy for ulcerative colitis. The patient is a twenty-three year old female with a known history of ulcerative colitis who was admitted with a week of worsening abdominal pain and increasing frequency of bloody bowel movements. Her condition was refractory to maximal medical therapy and a decision was made in conjunction with her gastroenterologist to pursue surgical therapy.

The surgeon pre-marks the ostomy site in the right lower quadrant. A small incision is made, followed by the use of a varus needle to insufflate the abdomen. Several trocars are then inserted in a standard fashion.

The surgeon explores the abdomen laparoscopically and begins dissection by taking the vessels of the diseased sigmoid colon medially. A mesenteric window is created, and dissection proceeds distally towards the recto sigmoid junction. A laparoscopic stapler is then used to transect the colon, leaving a rectal stump.
It is important to leave adequate length of rectal stump in order to facilitate the stage proctectomy, which will be performed approximately three months later.

The surgeon dissects along the descending colon towards the splenic flexure, initially taking the lateral attachments using a combination of energy and cutting. The mesentery is then dissected with an energy device, such as a ligature. The lesser sac is opened, and dissection proceeds from proximal to distal along the transverse colon, with the stomach visible to the left, the pancreas below, and the spleen ahead. Blunt dissection and energy are used to meet the prior dissection at the splenocolic ligament, completing the mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon focuses on the hepatic flexure, with the distal portion of the stomach visible to the right and the gallbladder ahead. Attention is then directed to the cecum, where the ileocolic pedicle is prepared for transection using a ligature device. The pulsating vessel is visible ahead of the device, and enlarged lymph nodes are noted around the ileocolic pedicle, indicative of inflammatory bowel disease.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the mesentery of the right colon, ensuring clear identification and avoidance of the duodenum and right ureter during the procedure.
The surgeon transects the remaining lateral attachments of the colon, leaving the transverse colonic mesentery visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects and identifies the cut edge of the mesentery near the duodenum. The mesentery is then transected starting at the proximal transverse colon and extending distally towards the splenic flexure. The ligature device is used to maintain hemostasis, and the final piece of mesentery is controlled, completing the laparoscopic dissection of the colon.

The surgeon places a small wound protector at the future ostomy site and secures it. The colon is carefully extracted through this area, ensuring no spillage or contamination occurs. A stapler is fired across the terminal ileum, and the specimen is removed from the surgical field.
The video shows a classic example of ulcerative colitis, with severe inflammation and disease affecting the distal colon, while the proximal colon appears relatively spared.
The surgeon constructs the end ileostomy using the Brook technique, turning the end of the ileum back and suturing it to the skin to create a smooth, rounded, inside-out stoma.
The video shows the final result of the ileostomy with the ostomy bag covering the stoma on the abdominal wall. A temporary drain is visible to the side of the ostomy bag.

The patient did very well and was discharged home on a soft diet within four days. She returned for laparoscopic completion proctectomy and J-pouch creation approximately three months later after stopping all of her medications and regaining her strength.
